3A_Bibliographic.citation: Chu, J., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., Al-shoaibi, A. A., Jackson, D. B., Rodgers, R. F., He, J., Baker, F. C., & Nagata, J. M. (2024). Screen time, problematic screen use, and eating disorder symptoms among early adolescents: findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study. Eating and Weight Disorders - Studies on Anorexia, Bulimia and Obesity, 29, 57.
3A_Bibliographic.journal: Eating and Weight Disorders - Studies on Anorexia, Bulimia and Obesity

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Data analyzed are from the ABCD 4.0 release for the baseline (2016-2018, 9-10 years old), year one (2017-2019) and year two (2018-2020) assessments. Participants with missing data for screen time and eating disorder symptoms were excluded (N=1,552, 13.1%).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Baseline total screen time and social media use (continuous hours/day); Year 2 problematic social media use (SMAQ) and problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ)
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey (baseline); Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ, Year 2); Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ, Year 2)
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, social media, mobile phone use
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day for screen time; continuous sum scores for SMAQ (1-6 scale) and MPIQ (1-7 scale)
3C_Exposure.time_frame: Typical weekday and weekend day (baseline); past year (Year 2 problematic use)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Total screen time and social media use were determined using the self-reported ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. We calculated a weighted average of the participants' typical weekday and weekend screen time use, ((weekday x 5) + (weekend x 2))/7, to report a single typical hours per day measure for each modality. Starting in year two, the ABCD Study utilized the Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ) and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ) to assess problematic use.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Eating disorder symptoms at Year 2 (fear of obesity, self-worth tied to weight, compensatory behaviors, binge eating, distress with binge eating)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5)
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: Fear of obesity: 1.4%; Self-worth tied to weight: 1.6%; Compensatory behaviors: 0.7%; Binge eating: 7.5%; Distress with binge eating: 2.9%
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The ABCD Study utilized the Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5) to assess eating disorder symptoms at two-year follow-up. Eating disorder symptoms included fear of weight gain, self-worth tied to weight, engaging in compensatory behaviors, binge eating, and distress with binge eating.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple logistic regression with propensity weights
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: race/ethnicity, sex, household income, parent education, site, baseline parent-reported eating disorder symptom, baseline BMI percentile, baseline anxiety symptoms, baseline BAS reward responsiveness
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple logistic regression analyses were conducted using Stata 18.0 to (1) estimate prospective associations between screen time (exposure variable) and the presence of adolescent-reported eating disorder symptoms at two-year follow-up, adjusting for confounders including parent-reported baseline eating disorder symptoms. Propensity weights developed by the ABCD Study were applied.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Total screen time: OR 1.12 (1.08-1.17), p<0.001 for fear of obesity
Total screen time: OR 1.10 (1.06-1.15), p<0.001 for self-worth tied to weight
Total screen time: OR 1.08 (1.05-1.11), p<0.001 for binge eating
Total screen time: OR 1.05 (1.01-1.09), p=0.011 for distress with binge eating
Problematic social media use: OR 1.75 (1.45-2.10), p<0.001 for self-worth tied to weight
Problematic mobile phone use: OR 1.82 (1.57-2.12), p<0.001 for distress with binge eating

3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: Despite the large sample size, participants in the study represent adolescents only within the US, which limits generalizability
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: it is important to note that the effect sizes of the associations between screen time and eating disorder symptoms were relatively small. However, they are reported for each additional hour, and thus, greater exposure may result in higher odds of developing symptoms.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper acknowledges small ORs for total screen time (1.05-1.12 per hour) but uses per-unit framing to argue accumulation. Problematic use measures show larger ORs (1.26-1.82) but are cross-sectional.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Clinicians should consider assessing for problem screen use and, when high, screen for disordered eating. Clinicians are encouraged to regularly assess screen time in youth, given the accumulating support for its association with a range of poor mental health outcomes.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: they are reported for each additional hour, and thus, greater exposure may result in higher odds of developing symptoms
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion acknowledges effect sizes are relatively small for total screen time but uses per-unit framing to imply accumulation. Problematic screen use measures show larger cross-sectional ORs. The paper recommends clinicians screen for disordered eating when screen use is high, despite the prospective total-screen-time ORs being very close to 1.0 (1.05-1.12 per hour). The discussion emphasizes body image mechanisms (social comparison, idealized bodies on social media) as plausible pathways.
